industries.name: Transportation & Aerospace
uses: Aircraft fuselage skin, wing panels, floor beams, and bulkheads in 2xxx (Al-Cu) and 7xxx (Al-Zn-Mg) alloys — aluminum comprises ~70–80% of a modern commercial aircraft's structural weight
Automotive body panels, hoods, doors, and trunk lids in 6xxx series aluminum sheet — each kilogram of steel replaced by aluminum saves ~2 kg vehicle weight and reduces lifetime fuel consumption
Aluminum space frames and structural extrusions in premium vehicles (Audi A8, Jaguar XJ) and all-aluminum body trucks (Ford F-150 since 2015)
Ship superstructures, naval vessel hulls, and high-speed ferry hulls where corrosion resistance and weight savings offset higher material cost versus steel
Railway passenger car bodies, metro car shells, and high-speed train structures (Shinkansen, TGV) using extruded aluminum profiles and welded assemblies

companies.name: Constellium
companies.use: Leading aerospace aluminum producer. Supplies 7xxx and 2xxx alloy sheet, plate, and extrusions to Airbus and Boeing for fuselage, wing, and structural applications. Also major automotive aluminum sheet supplier.

companies.name: Novelis (Hindalco)
companies.use: World's largest aluminum rolling company. Supplies aluminum body sheet to Ford (F-150 body), Jaguar Land Rover, Audi, and other OEMs. Also produces aerospace plate and can body stock.

companies.name: Kaiser Aluminum
companies.use: Major US producer of aerospace and automotive aluminum plate, sheet, and extrusions. Supplies structural aluminum to Boeing, Lockheed Martin, and automotive Tier 1 suppliers.

companies.name: Aleris (now Novelis)
companies.use: Specialty aerospace aluminum plate producer supplying 7xxx series material to Airbus A350, Boeing 787, and military aircraft programs. Acquired by Novelis in 2020.

industries.name: Packaging (Beverage Cans & Foil)
uses: Beverage can body stock (3004 alloy: Al-Mn-Mg) and lid/tab stock (5182 alloy: Al-Mg) — the aluminum beverage can is the world's most recycled packaging format, with ~70% recycling rate in the US
Household aluminum foil (1xxx series, 99%+ pure) for food storage, cooking, and pharmaceutical packaging — produced by rolling to 6–25 micron thickness
Flexible food packaging laminates: aluminum foil bonded to plastic film for coffee pods, retort pouches, and yogurt lids providing oxygen and moisture barrier
Pharmaceutical blister packs: aluminum foil heat-sealed to PVC forming individual dose cavities for tablets and capsules — provides tamper evidence and moisture protection
Aerosol cans for deodorant, hairspray, and shaving cream; aluminum tubes for pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and food pastes

companies.name: Ball Corporation
companies.use: World's largest aluminum beverage can manufacturer. Produces cans for Budweiser, Coca-Cola, PepsiCo, and hundreds of craft brewers across Americas, Europe, and Middle East. Major driver of aluminum recycling programs.

companies.name: Crown Holdings
companies.use: Second-largest aluminum can manufacturer globally. Produces beverage cans across 47 countries and aluminum aerosol cans for consumer goods brands. Operates extensive can recycling infrastructure.

companies.name: Ardagh Metal Packaging
companies.use: Major European aluminum beverage can producer supplying brewers and soft drink companies. Produced approximately 8 billion cans annually from plants across Europe and the Americas.

companies.name: Novelis (Hindalco)
companies.share: ~50% of North American can sheet market
companies.use: Dominant supplier of aluminum can body and lid sheet to Ball, Crown, and other can makers. Its recycled content can sheet (Evercan) uses up to 90% recycled aluminum.

industries.name: Construction & Architecture
uses: Window frames, curtain wall systems, storefronts, and door frames in 6063 extruded aluminum — corrosion resistant, paintable, and thermally breakable for energy efficiency
Roofing and cladding panels in aluminum composite material (ACM) and corrugated aluminum for commercial buildings, warehouses, and industrial facilities
Structural bridge decks in aluminum alloy plate — lighter than steel, eliminating need for bridge strengthening and extending structural life
Scaffolding and access equipment: aluminum tube and fitting systems 60% lighter than steel equivalents, reducing worker fatigue and transport costs

Electrical transmission lines: ACSR (aluminum conductor steel-reinforced) uses aluminum for conductivity with a steel core for tensile strength — aluminum conducts at 61% of copper's conductivity but at one-third the weight

companies.name: Hydro Building Systems
companies.use: Leading supplier of aluminum window, door, and curtain wall systems to European construction markets. Produces extruded aluminum profiles for architects and contractors under the Wicona, Technal, and Domal brands.

companies.name: Arconic (formerly Alcoa)
companies.use: Produces aluminum architectural products including Reynobond aluminum composite panels for building facades. Supplies extruded and rolled aluminum to construction, automotive, and aerospace sectors.

companies.name: Sapa (now Hydro Extrusions)
companies.use: World's largest aluminum extrusion company (now part of Norsk Hydro). Produces custom and standard aluminum profiles for construction, automotive, and industrial customers from 100+ plants globally.

industries.name: Electrical & Energy Infrastructure
uses: Overhead power transmission lines: ACSR, AAAC, and ACAR aluminum conductors carry the vast majority of the world's high-voltage electricity — aluminum's low weight allows longer spans with fewer towers

Aluminum busbars and switchgear components in electrical substations and distribution equipment — aluminum's conductivity, weight, and corrosion resistance make it preferred over copper above 35mm² cross-section
Transformer windings in distribution transformers increasingly use aluminum wire as a lower-cost substitute for copper — accepted where weight and efficiency meet specifications
Solar panel frames in anodized 6063 aluminum alloy — provides structural support, grounding path, and corrosion resistance for rooftop and utility-scale PV installations
Heat sinks for power electronics, LED lighting drivers, and computer CPUs — aluminum's thermal conductivity (237 W/m·K) and machinability make it the dominant heat management material

companies.name: Prysmian Group
companies.use: World's largest cable manufacturer. Produces aluminum overhead transmission conductors, aluminum-core power cables, and submarine cables for electrical grid infrastructure globally.

companies.name: Nexans
companies.use: Major producer of aluminum overhead power lines, underground cables, and industrial cables. Supplies grid operators, renewable energy projects, and industrial customers across 40+ countries.

companies.name: General Cable (now Prysmian)
companies.use: Major North American aluminum wire and cable manufacturer, acquired by Prysmian in 2018. Produced aluminum building wire, overhead conductors, and utility cables for the US electrical grid.

industries.name: Primary Aluminum Smelting
uses: Hall-Héroult electrolytic smelting dissolves alumina (Al₂O₃) in molten cryolite (Na₃AlF₆) at ~960°C and applies 4–5 volts DC to reduce Al³⁺ to liquid aluminum metal — each tonne of aluminum requires ~13,500 kWh of electricity
Bayer process refines bauxite ore to alumina: bauxite digested in hot NaOH dissolves aluminum as sodium aluminate, which is then precipitated as aluminum hydroxide and calcined to Al₂O₃
Secondary (recycled) aluminum smelting requires only ~5% of the energy of primary production — aluminum recycling is one of the highest-value recycling processes in the world
Aluminum alloying: primary aluminum (99.7%+ pure) is combined with copper, manganese, silicon, magnesium, and zinc at casthouse to produce the ~400 commercial aluminum alloys
Anode manufacturing: primary smelters consume ~400 kg of carbon (petroleum coke) anodes per tonne of aluminum produced — anodes oxidize during electrolysis and must be replaced regularly

companies.name: China Hongqiao Group
companies.use: World's largest primary aluminum producer. Operates integrated coal-power and smelting complexes in Shandong and Yunnan provinces. China as a whole produces ~57% of world primary aluminum.

companies.name: Rusal (En+ Group)
companies.use: World's largest aluminum producer outside China. Operates smelters powered by Siberian hydroelectricity — among the lowest carbon-intensity primary aluminum in the world.

companies.name: Alcoa Corporation
companies.use: Pioneer of Hall-Héroult process (1888). Operates primary smelters in Australia, Canada, Iceland, Norway, and Spain. Also world's largest bauxite miner and third-largest alumina refiner.

companies.name: Norsk Hydro
companies.use: Integrated aluminum company from bauxite to finished products. Operates hydropower-based smelters in Norway and Brazil with among the lowest CO₂ intensity in the global industry.
